title: Scottish tablet
subtitle: Hard, grainy condensed milk candy — firmer and more crystalline than fudge
excerpt: A traditional Scottish confection cooked to the firm-ball stage and beaten until thick and grainy. Firmer and sweeter than fudge, with a crumbly, melt-on-the-tongue texture. Makes about 50 pieces.
sourceNotes: Scottish tablet is documented in Scottish household recipe collections from the early eighteenth century. It appears in Mistress Margaret Dods, The Cook and Housewife's Manual (1826), as a standard Scottish confection made from sugar and cream cooked to a hard set. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), records tablet as a regional Scottish candy distinct from English fudge. The key distinction is a higher cooking temperature and a larger crystal size from less beating, producing a harder, more granular texture.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap pieces individually and freeze. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles in a larger pan — use a large, deep saucepan as the mixture boils up.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for 3 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Cooked on the stovetop to 118–120°C (firm-ball stage) on a sugar thermometer

glossaryTerms.definition: Scottish tablet is deliberately grainy — that is the texture it is supposed to have. The large crystals form because tablet is cooked to a higher temperature than fudge (118–120°C rather than 115°C) and beaten less. More beating breaks crystals down smaller, giving fudge its smooth, creamy texture. Less beating, or beating from a higher temperature, produces the characteristic crumbly, sandy grain of tablet. The mixture sets faster than fudge — once it starts to thicken and go opaque in the pan, work quickly.

content.text: Tablet is not failed fudge. The
is the correct texture: crumbly and melting — exactly as it should be. The mixture sets fast once it starts to go. Have the tin ready lined before you start cooking, and don't leave the pan.

Line a 30 × 20 cm tray with baking paper. Combine the {{caster-sugar}}, {{condensed-milk}}, {{whole-milk}}, and {{unsalted-butter}} in a large, heavy-based saucepan. Heat gently, stirring, until the sugar dissolves. Bring to a boil and cook at a steady boil, stirring frequently, until the mixture reaches 118–120°C on a {{sugar-thermometer}} — the firm-ball stage. Remove from the heat. Stir in the {{vanilla-extract}}.

Beat the mixture vigorously with a wooden spoon until it begins to go from glossy to matte and thicken visibly — about 2–3 minutes. Unlike fudge, tablet sets quickly, so do not wait too long. As soon as it starts to hold its shape and look grainy around the edges, pour immediately into the prepared tray. Smooth the surface with the back of the spoon. Mark into squares with a knife before it fully sets; this takes about 5 minutes. Leave to set completely at room temperature for 2 hours, then break along the score lines.
